title: Bond Yield Calculator: YTM & Duration
description: Calculate bond yield to maturity (YTM), current yield, Macaulay and modified duration with this interactive bond yield calculator.

schemas.headline: Bond Yield Calculator: YTM & Duration
schemas.description: Calculate bond yield to maturity (YTM), current yield, Macaulay and modified duration with this interactive bond yield calculator.

mainEntity.name: How do you calculate yield to maturity on a bond?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yield to maturity (YTM) is calculated by solving the bond pricing equation for the discount rate that equates the present value of all future cash flows (coupons and principal) to the current market price of the bond. This is typically done using numerical methods like the Newton-Raphson or bisection method.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between current yield and yield to maturity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Current yield is simply the annual coupon payment divided by the current bond price, ignoring capital gains or losses at maturity. Yield to maturity (YTM) accounts for the total return, including interest payments and the difference between the current price and the face value received at maturity.

mainEntity.name: How does bond price affect yield?

acceptedAnswer.text: Bond prices and yields have an inverse relationship. When a bond's price falls below its face value (discount), its yield increases. When the price rises above its face value (premium), its yield decreases.

mainEntity.name: What is modified duration in bond investing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Modified duration measures a bond's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. It represents the approximate percentage change in a bond's price for a 1% change in yield. A higher duration means the bond's price is more volatile when interest rates move.

Convexity measures the curvature in the relationship between bond prices and yields, capturing how duration changes as yields change. It provides a more accurate estimate of price changes for larger shifts in interest rates, showing that bond prices rise more when yields fall than they drop when yields rise by the same amount.
